blocks:
Ayahs

وَٱلْعَـٰدِيَـٰتِ ضَبْحًا (١)
By the snorting chargers, (1)
فَٱلْمُورِيَـٰتِ قَدْحًا (٢)
by the strikers of sparks [with their hoofs], (2)
فَٱلْمُغِيرَٰتِ صُبْحًا (٣)
by the raiders at dawn, (3)
فَأَثَرْنَ بِهِۦ نَقْعًا (٤)
raising therein a trail of dust, (4)
فَوَسَطْنَ بِهِۦ جَمْعًا (٥)
and cleaving therein a host! (5)
إِنَّ ٱلْإِنسَـٰنَ لِرَبِّهِۦ لَكَنُودٌ (٦)
Indeed man is ungrateful to his Lord, (6)
وَإِنَّهُۥ عَلَىٰ ذَٰلِكَ لَشَهِيدٌ (٧)
and indeed he is [himself] witness to that! (7)
وَإِنَّهُۥ لِحُبِّ ٱلْخَيْرِ لَشَدِيدٌ (٨)
And indeed he is an avid lover of wealth. (8)
۞ أَفَلَا يَعْلَمُ إِذَا بُعْثِرَ مَا فِى ٱلْقُبُورِ (٩)
Does he not know, when what is [buried] in the graves is turned over, (9)
وَحُصِّلَ مَا فِى ٱلصُّدُورِ (١٠)
and what is [concealed] in the breasts is divulged, (10)
إِنَّ رَبَّهُم بِهِمْ يَوْمَئِذٍ لَّخَبِيرٌۢ (١١)
on that day their Lord will be well informed about them [and their deeds]? (11)

Content and Virtue of Surah al-Adiyat

There is a vigorous debate among commentators as to whether this Surah was revealed in "Makkah" or "Madinah"; many have considered it "Makki," while a group regards it as "Madani."

The brevity of the verse segments, the reliance on oaths, and the emphasis on the issue of the Resurrection are indications that support the Surah being Makki.

On the other hand, the content of the oaths in this Surah, which bears a strong resemblance to matters of Jihad—as will be explained in detail, Allah willing—and also the narrations stating that this Surah was revealed after the Battle of "Dhat al-Salasil," point toward it being Madani.

(The battle occurred in the eighth year of the Hijrah, in which a large group of disbelievers were taken captive and bound tightly with ropes, hence it was named "Dhat al-Salasil" [The Battle of the Chains]; its details will come in the commentary of the verses, God willing.) This serves as evidence for the Madani origin of this Surah. Even if we consider the opening oaths of this Surah as referring to the movement of the pilgrims toward Mina and Mash'ar, it still aligns with Madinah.

It is true that the Hajj rituals, with most of their details, were prevalent among the pre-Islamic Arabs—following the tradition of Ibrahim—but they were so mixed with superstitions that it seems unlikely the Quran would swear by them in that state.

Taking all these aspects into account, we prefer the view that it is "Madani."

From what we have mentioned, the content of the Surah also becomes clear: in the beginning, it mentions awakening oaths, then it speaks of some of the weaknesses of the human species such as ingratitude, stinginess, and worldliness, and finally, it concludes the Surah with a brief and eloquent reference to the issue of the Resurrection and Allah's scientific encompassment of His servants.

Regarding the virtue of reciting this Surah, it is narrated from the Holy Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله:

"Whoever recites it will be given ten rewards for every pilgrim who stays at 'Muzdalifah' (on the night of Eid al-Adha) and is present there." [^1] and [^2] In another tradition from Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام, we read:

"Whoever recites Surah al-Adiyat and makes its recitation a habit, Allah will resurrect him specifically with Amir al-Mu'minin (peace be upon him) on the Day of Judgment, and he will be in his company and among his friends." [^3] It is also understood from some narrations that Surah al-Adiyat is equivalent to half of the Quran. [^4] It goes without saying that all this virtue is for those who make it a program for their lives, believe in its entire content, and act upon it.

[^1]: "Jam'" is one of the names of "Mash'ar al-Haram," because people gather there, or because the Maghrib and Isha prayers are performed there together without a gap. [^2] and [^3]: "Majma' al-Bayan," Vol. 10, p. 527. [^4]: "Durr al-Manthur," Vol. 6, p. 383.

بِسْمِ اللَّهِ الرَّحْمنِ الرَّحِيمِ‌

وَ الْعادِياتِ ضَبْحاً (1) فَالْمُورِياتِ قَدْحاً (2) فَالْمُغِيراتِ صُبْحاً (3) فَأَثَرْنَ بِهِ نَقْعاً (4)

فَوَسَطْنَ بِهِ جَمْعاً (5) إِنَّ الْإِنْسانَ لِرَبِّهِ لَكَنُودٌ (6) وَ إِنَّهُ عَلى‌ ذلِكَ لَشَهِيدٌ (7) وَ إِنَّهُ لِحُبِّ الْخَيْرِ لَشَدِيدٌ (8) أَ فَلا يَعْلَمُ إِذا بُعْثِرَ ما فِي الْقُبُورِ (9)

وَ حُصِّلَ ما فِي الصُّدُورِ (10) إِنَّ رَبَّهُمْ بِهِمْ يَوْمَئِذٍ لَخَبِيرٌ (11)

Occasion of Revelation

It is mentioned in a tradition that this Surah was revealed after the Battle of "Dhat al-Salasil," and the event was as follows:

In the eighth year of the Hijrah, the Holy Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله was informed that twelve thousand horsemen had gathered in the land of "Yabis" and had covenanted with one another not to rest until they had killed the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله and Ali عليه السلام and dismantled the Muslim community! The Holy Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله sent a large group of his companions under the leadership of some Sahaba to confront them, but they returned without result after some negotiations. Finally, the Holy Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله dispatched Ali عليه السلام with a large group of the Muhajirun and Ansar to fight them. They moved swiftly toward the enemy's region, traveling by night, and surrounded the enemy at dawn. First, they offered them Islam; when they refused, while it was still dark, they attacked them and crushed them, killing some, taking their women and children captive, and seizing much wealth as booty.

Surah "al-Adiyat" was revealed while the soldiers of Islam had not yet returned to Madinah. The Prophet of Allah صلى الله عليه وآله came for the morning prayer that day and recited this Surah in the prayer. After the prayer ended, the companions said: "This is a Surah we have never heard before!"

He said: "Yes, Ali عليه السلام has triumphed over the enemies, and Jibril brought me this Surah last night as glad tidings."

A few days later, Ali عليه السلام entered Madinah with the spoils and captives. [^1] Some believe that this is one of the clear instances of the verse, not its specific occasion of revelation.

[^1]: Bihar al-Anwar, Vol. 21, p. 66 onwards; "Majma' al-Bayan," Vol. 10, p. 528; and some other histories.

Commentary

Swearing by the Awakened Jihadists!

We said that this Surah begins with awakening oaths. First, He says:

"By the panting chargers (that run toward the field of Jihad)" (وَ الْعادِياتِ ضَبْحاً) [^1] or by the camels of the pilgrims that move panting from the land of Arafat to Mash'ar al-Haram and from Mash'ar toward Mina.

"Adiyat" is the plural of "Adiyah," from the root "Adw" (on the weight of Sabr), which originally means passing and separating, whether it be in the heart, which is called "Adawat" (enmity), or in physical movement, which is called "Adw" (running), and sometimes in transactions, which is called "Udwan" (transgression). Here, it refers to running with speed.

"Dabh" (on the weight of Madh) refers to the sound of the horse's heavy and rapid breathing heard while it is running.

As we indicated above, there are two views in the interpretation of this verse:

First, that it refers to the horses that rush toward the field of Jihad, and since Jihad is a sacred matter, these animals also gain such value in this sacred path that they are worthy of being sworn by.

[^1]: Normally, it should have been said "wal-adiyati adwan," but since panting (dabh) is a necessary consequence of running (adw), "dabh" was used instead. Some have also said the verse has a hidden clause and is estimated as "wal-adiyati yadhbahna dabhun."

The other interpretation is swearing by the camels that move swiftly during the great obligation of Hajj between the stations and sacred places, and for this reason, they possess a sanctity worthy of an oath.

In a tradition, Ibn Abbas says: "I was in the Hijr of Ismail next to the Kaaba when a man came and asked me about the verse 'وَ الْعادِياتِ ضَبْحاً'. I said: 'It refers to the horses that charge in the way of Jihad and return to their resting place at night, and the soldiers who light fires and prepare food for themselves.'"

That man left me and went to Ali ibn Abi Talib عليه السلام while he was by the Zamzam and asked him about the same verse.

He said: "Have you asked anyone about this verse before me?" He replied: "Yes, I asked Ibn Abbas, and he said it refers to the horses that charge in the way of Jihad." He said: "Go and call him to come to me."

When I came before Hazrat Ali عليه السلام, he said: "Why do you give fatwas to the people about that which you do not know? The first ghazwa in Islam was Badr, and we had only two horses: a horse belonging to Zubayr and a horse belonging to Miqdad. How can 'Adiyat' mean 'horses'?! No, it refers to the camels that go from 'Arafat' to 'Mash'ar' and from 'Mash'ar' to 'Mina'."

Ibn Abbas says: "When I heard this, I turned back from my opinion and accepted the view of Amir al-Mu'minin Ali عليه السلام." [^1] It is also possible that "Adiyat" has a broad meaning that includes both the horses of the mujahideen and the camels of the pilgrims, and the purpose of the above narration is that its meaning should not be limited to horses because that meaning is not applicable everywhere; its clearer instance is the camels of the pilgrims.

This interpretation seems more appropriate in several respects.

Then He adds: "By those that strike sparks of fire" (فَالْمُورِياتِ قَدْحاً). The horses of the mujahideen that move so swiftly toward the battlefield that sparks fly from the impact of their hooves against the desert stones, or the camels that run swiftly to the stations of Hajj, and stones and pebbles fly from under their feet, producing sparks upon hitting other stones.

Or the tribes and groups that light fires at the Hajj stations to prepare food, or it is a metaphor for those who kindle the fire of war and Jihad, or the tongues that strike fire in the hearts of the enemy with their crushing speech, or it refers to those who, according to some commentators, strive to fulfill the needs of the people and advance their goals just as fire comes out of a flintstone. But these possibilities seem very remote, and the apparent meaning of the verse is the first two interpretations.

"Muriyat" is the plural of "Muriyah," from the root "Ira'," meaning to kindle fire, and "Qadh" means striking stone, wood, or iron and flint against each other to produce a spark.

Then in the third oath, He says: "By those that raid at dawn" (فَالْمُغِيراتِ صُبْحاً).

The custom of the Arabs—as Tabarsi says in "Majma' al-Bayan"—was to go near the enemy's region at night and lie in ambush to attack at dawn.

In the occasion of revelation of these verses (or one of its clear instances), we read that the soldiers of Islam, under the command of Ali عليه السلام, used the darkness of the night to advance toward the battlefield and lay in ambush near the enemy tribe, then raided them like a lightning bolt at dawn, breaking their power before the enemy could show any reaction.

And if we consider the oaths as referring to the camels of the pilgrims, the meaning of this verse is the rush of the camel caravans at the dawn of Eid from "Mash'ar" to "Mina."

"Mughirat" is the plural of "Mughirah," from the root "Igharah," meaning to raid and attack the enemy. Since this raid and attack sometimes occur for the purpose of taking property, this word is sometimes used in the sense common in Persian, meaning to plunder and take the property of others.

Some have said that in the root of this word, the idea of raiding and attacking with horses is embedded, but the instances of its use clearly show that if this restriction existed at the beginning, it was gradually removed.

And the possibility suggested by some that "Mughirat" here refers to "raiding tribes and groups" moving toward the battlefield or rushing toward Mina seems remote, because the verse "وَ الْعادِياتِ ضَبْحاً" was certainly a description for the horses or camels, not their owners, and this verse is a continuation of that.

Then He points to another characteristic of these mujahideen and their mounts, adding: "They attack the enemy so swiftly that they stir up dust thereby" (فَأَثَرْنَ بِهِ نَقْعاً) [^1] or that due to the rush of the pilgrims' camels from Mash'ar al-Haram toward Mina, dust is scattered in every direction.

"Atharna" is from the root "Itharah," meaning to scatter "dust" or "smoke," and it is sometimes used to mean "exciting" or "arousing." It also sometimes refers to the spreading of sound waves in space.

"Naq'" (on the weight of Naf') means "dust," and the origin of this root means the sinking of water or sinking into water, and since sinking into "dust" bears a resemblance to it, this word has been applied to it. "Naqi'" is used for stagnant water.

And in the last of their characteristics, He says: "Then they appeared in the midst of the enemy at that morning" (فَوَسَطْنَ بِهِ جَمْعاً) [^2]. Their attack was so unexpected and lightning-fast that in a few moments they split the enemy's ranks, raided their heart, and dismantled their gathering. This is the result of that speed of action, alertness, readiness, bravery, and courage.

Or it refers to the entry of the pilgrims from "Mash'ar" into the heart of "Mina."

Some have also said: it means placing the enemy in a circle of encirclement, but this interpretation is only correct if the word "fawasatna" were read with a shadda on the 'sin', whereas the famous recitation is not so. Therefore, the first meaning is the correct one.

Altogether, in a final summary, we reach the point that oaths are sworn by horses that first move swiftly and panting toward the field of Jihad, then their speed increases such that sparks rise from the impact of their hooves on stones, piercing the darkness of the night. In the next stage, when they reach the vicinity of the enemy's region, they take them by surprise and raid them as the light of day appears—a raid that scatters dust in the air—and finally, they enter the heart of the enemy's ranks, dismantling their lines and achieving a brilliant victory.

Swear by these powerful horses! Swear by these brave riders! Swear by the breaths of the mujahideen's mounts! Swear by those fiery sparks that rise from their hooves! Swear by their surprise raid! Swear by those particles of dust they scatter in the air!

And finally, swear by their entry into the heart of the enemy's ranks and their dismantling of them and their brilliant victory! Although all that has been said is not explicitly in the wording of these oaths, they are all included in the implicit indication of the speech.

From here, it becomes clear that Jihad has such greatness that even the breaths of the mujahideen's horses are worthy of an oath, as are the sparks resulting from their hooves hitting the desert stones, and the dust they scatter in the air. Yes, the dust of the battlefield of Jihad is also highly valuable and great.

Some have said that these oaths possibly refer to souls that can transfer their perfections to others, manifest sparks of knowledge with their thoughts, raid whims and desires, scatter divine longing in themselves and others, and finally take their place in the heart of the inhabitants of Illiyyin. [^1] But it is clear that these cannot be accepted as the interpretation of the above verses; rather, they are metaphors that come to mind in accordance with the interpretation of the verse.

[^1]: The pronoun in "bihi" refers to "adw" (running) which is understood from the phrase "wal-adiyati dabhun," thus the 'ba' in it means "causality," meaning because of this running, dust fills the air. Some have also suggested it refers to the time or place in which this galloping occurs, thus the "ba" would mean "container/circumstance," but the first meaning is the correct one. [^2]: Regarding the reference of the pronoun "bihi" and the meaning of the 'ba' here, the matter is the same as stated in the previous verse. [^1]: Tafsir al-Baydawi, p. 465.

After these great oaths, He proceeds to the response of the oath—that is, the thing for which the oaths were sworn—saying: "Indeed, man is ungrateful and stingy toward the blessings of his Lord" (إِنَّ الْإِنْسانَ لِرَبِّهِ لَكَنُودٌ). The same unrefined man, the same man upon whose heart the lights of divine knowledge and the teachings of the prophets have not shone, and ultimately the same man who has surrendered himself to rebellious instincts and desires; he is certainly "ungrateful" and "stingy."

"Kanud" is said of land from which nothing grows, and it is also applied to an ungrateful and stingy person.

Commentators have given many meanings for "Kanud"; Abu al-Futuh al-Razi has narrated about fifteen meanings in this regard, but they are mostly branches of the same original meaning we mentioned above, including:

  1. A Kanud is one who counts his misfortunes with great detail but forgets the blessings.
  2. A Kanud is one who eats God's blessings alone and withholds them from others, as we read in a tradition from the Holy Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله: "Do you know who the Kanud is?" They said: "Allah and His Messenger know best." He said: "The Kanud is the one who eats alone, withholds his gift, and beats his slave." [^1]
  3. A Kanud is one who does not empathize with his friends in difficulties and misfortunes.
  4. One whose goodness is very little.
  5. One who, when a blessing reaches him, withholds it from others, and if he is afflicted with a problem, he becomes impatient and wails.
  6. One who spends divine blessings in sin.
  7. One who denies the blessing of God.

But as we said, all these meanings are instances and branches of the same ingratitude and stinginess.

[^1]: "Majma' al-Bayan," Vol. 10, p. 530.

The expression "man" (al-insan) in such cases refers to wicked, desire-following, rebellious, and transgressive humans, and some have interpreted it as the disbelieving man; otherwise, certainly not every human is like this. There are many whose souls are intertwined with gratitude and giving, and who loathe ingratitude and stinginess—humans who, in the light of faith in God, have stepped out of the valley of selfishness and self-worship and have taken flight in the sky of knowing the names and attributes of the Lord and adopting divine ethics.

Then He adds: "And indeed, he himself is a witness to that" (وَ إِنَّهُ عَلى‌ ذلِكَ لَشَهِيدٌ). Because man has insight into his own self, and if he can hide his inner qualities from everyone, he cannot hide them from God and his own conscience, whether he confesses to this truth or not!

Some have also said the pronoun in "innahu" refers to "God," meaning Allah is a witness to the existence of the quality of Kanud in man.

However, considering the preceding and succeeding verses where similar pronouns refer to man, this possibility seems very remote, even though many commentators have preferred this interpretation.

The possibility has also been raised that it refers to man's testimony against his own sins and faults on the Day of Judgment, as understood from many verses of the Quran.

This interpretation also has no specific evidence here, for the verse has a broad concept that includes his testimony and witness to his own ingratitude and stinginess in this world as well.

It is true that sometimes man is unable to recognize himself and, so to speak, deceives his own conscience, and the satanic "taswil" (embellishment) and "tazyin" (decoration) make his reprehensible qualities appear beautiful in his eyes; but in the specific case of ingratitude and stinginess, the matter is so clear that he cannot cover it up or deceive his conscience.

Again, the verse adds: "And indeed, he is intense in his love of wealth and material things" (وَ إِنَّهُ لِحُبِّ الْخَيْرِ لَشَدِيدٌ) [^1]. And this intense and excessive love for wealth and riches is the cause of his stinginess and ingratitude.

Of course, "Khayr" (good) has a broad meaning that includes every kind of goodness, and certainly, the love for many good things such as knowledge, piety, Paradise, and happiness is not a reprehensible thing for the Quran to criticize with the above expression. Therefore, commentators have interpreted it here to mean "wealth," which is supported both by the context of the passage and the previous verse, as well as some other verses of the Quran, such as verse 180 of Surah al-Baqarah which says: "It is prescribed for you, when death approaches one of you, if he leaves behind wealth (khayran), to make a testament for parents and near relatives."

Certainly, the application of "Khayr" to "wealth" is because it is a good thing in its essence and can be a means for various types of good deeds, but the ungrateful and stingy man diverts it from its original purpose and uses it in the path of selfishness and self-indulgence.

[^1]: The "lam" in "li-hubbi al-khayr" may be the "lam of transition" or the "lam of cause." According to the first, its interpretation is as mentioned above, and according to the second, the concept of the verse becomes: man is stingy because of the love of wealth. But certainly, the first interpretation is more appropriate.

Then, in the form of a rhetorical question coupled with a threat, He says: "Does this ungrateful, stingy, and world-worshipping man not know when all that is in the graves is resurrected...?" (أَ فَلا يَعْلَمُ إِذا بُعْثِرَ ما فِي الْقُبُورِ).

"And when that which is in the breasts—of disbelief and faith, sincerity and hypocrisy, pride and arrogance and humility, good and evil intentions—is made manifest": (وَ حُصِّلَ ما فِي الصُّدُورِ).

"On that day, their Lord is well-aware of them and their deeds and intentions" and will punish them accordingly (إِنَّ رَبَّهُمْ بِهِمْ يَوْمَئِذٍ لَخَبِيرٌ).

"Bu'thira" is from the root "bu'tharah" (on the weight of manqabah), which originally means turning upside down, bringing out, and extracting. Since at the time of the revival of the dead, the graves are turned upside down and what is inside them appears, this expression is used in the above verses regarding the Resurrection.

The expression "ma fil-qubur" (considering that "ma" is usually for non-rational beings) is either because it looks at the state when the dead are still dust, or because of the ambiguity surrounding them, as it is not known who they are.

The expression "qubur" (graves) does not contradict the fact that a group of people essentially have no graves—for example, those who drown in the sea, or those whose graves disappear after a while and their dust is scattered—because it refers to the majority of people who do have graves. Furthermore, "grave" here can have a broad meaning, i.e., the place where the dust of humans is located, even if it is not in the form of a conventional grave.

"Hussila" is from the root "tahsil," which originally means extracting the "kernel" from the "shell." It is also applied to the refining of minerals and the extraction of gold and the like from ore. Then it came to be used in a broad sense, meaning absolute extraction and separation. In the verse under discussion, it refers to the separation of the good and evil hidden in the hearts, whether it be faith and disbelief, or good and bad qualities, or good and evil intentions, which on that day will be separated from one another and made manifest and clear, and everyone will receive their reward or punishment accordingly.

Just as it is stated in verse 9 of Surah al-Tariq: "On the day when the secrets are tested (revealed)."

The expression "yawma'idhin" (on that day) and the emphasis on the fact that Allah "on that day" is aware of their deeds and the hidden secrets of their hearts—even though we know God is always aware of these matters—is because that day is the Day of Recompense, and He will recompense them for their deeds and beliefs.

According to some commentators, this expression is like someone saying to another in a threatening tone, "I will soon know your affair," while even today such knowledge exists; the meaning is that I will give you the result of it.

Yes, Allah is always and in every state fully aware of the secrets of the inner and outer, but the effect of this awareness becomes more apparent and manifest on the Day of Judgment and at the time of reward and punishment. This is a warning to all humans that if they truly believe in it, it will create a powerful barrier between them and sins, whether they be manifest or hidden, outer or inner sins, and the educational effect of this belief is hidden from no one.

Key Points

1. The Relationship Between the Oaths of this Surah and its Goal

One of the questions raised about this Surah is: what relationship exists between the oath by the horses of the mujahideen and the sentence "إِنَّ الْإِنْسانَ لِرَبِّهِ لَكَنُودٌ"?

For the study of Quranic verses shows us that there is always a kind of relationship between the oaths and the "muqsam bihi" (that which is sworn by), and essentially the eloquence and articulateness of the Quran require such a thing.

Regarding the verses under discussion, the relationship might be that the Quran says: there are self-sacrificing humans who advance fearlessly in the path of Jihad and do not withhold any sacrifice, giving their lives and wealth in the way of God; given this, how can some be so stingy and ungrateful, neither praising God for His blessings nor sacrificing in His way?

It is true that the oath is sworn by the horses, but we know that their importance is from the perspective that they are tools for the mujahideen; in reality, it is an oath by the Jihad of the mujahideen (similarly if the oath is by the camels of the pilgrims and visitors to the House of God).

Some have also said that the relationship is established because these animals move swiftly in the path of God's pleasure, so why do you, O man, not submit to Him, you who are the noblest of creatures and more worthy?! But the first suitability is clearer.

2. Is Human Nature Ingratitude and Stinginess?

Some might use the sentence "إِنَّ الْإِنْسانَ لِرَبِّهِ لَكَنُودٌ" to infer that the state of being "Kanud"—that is, ingratitude and stinginess—is part of the nature of all humans. Then the question arises: how can this be compatible with the awakened conscience and innate consciousness that invite man to thank the Benefactor and to self-sacrifice?

A similar question is raised regarding many verses of the Holy Quran that describe man with clear points of weakness.

In one place, man is considered "unjust and ignorant" (al-Ahzab: 72). In another place, "impatient/anxious" (al-Ma'arij: 19). In another place, "despairing and ungrateful" (Hud: 9). And in another place, "rebellious" (al-Alaq: 6).

Are all these weaknesses truly embedded in human nature? While the Quran explicitly states that God has honored the children of Adam and granted them superiority over all creatures: "And We have certainly honored the children of Adam and carried them on the land and sea and provided for them of the good things and preferred them over much of what We have created, with [definite] preference" (al-Isra: 70).

The answer to this question becomes clear by noting one point: man possesses two dimensions of existence, and for this reason, he can reach the "highest of the high" (A'la Illiyyin) in his ascending arc and be dragged to the "lowest of the low" (Asfal al-Safilin) in his descending arc.

If he comes under the training of divine educators, takes inspiration from the message of reason, and engages in self-purification, he becomes the instance of "And We preferred them over much of what We have created."

And if he turns his back on faith and piety and leaves the path of the friends of God, he becomes a being that is "unjust," "disbelieving," "despairing," "ungrateful," "impatient," and "stingy."

In this way, there is no contradiction between these verses; rather, each looks at one of the dimensions of human existence.

Yes, within human nature lie the roots of all goodness, virtues, honors, and excellences, just as man has the readiness for the opposite of these virtues. Therefore, no creature in the world of creation has such a distance between its ascending and descending arcs (reflect on this).

3. The Greatness of Jihad!

In the Holy Quran, the issue of Jihad and the greatness of the mujahideen in the way of God is mentioned frequently, but perhaps nowhere is the matter depicted with such greatness that even the panting of their horses, the sparks from the hooves of these mounts, and the dust resulting from their rapid movement are recognized as so great that they are made the subject of an oath.

Specifically, emphasis is placed on their speed of action, which is one of the most important factors for victory in wars, and also on the principle of surprise, which is another factor for success in war.

All these are teachings in the field of the "Jihad" program.

It is noteworthy that in the occasion of revelation of this Surah, it is also mentioned that Ali عليه السلام ordered the mounts to be prepared in the darkness of the night, given the necessary fodder, and saddled, and to be in a state of full readiness. When the veil of the night's darkness was pierced, he immediately performed the morning prayer with his companions and attacked the enemy without delay. The enemy woke up only when they were under the feet of the horses of the mujahideen of Islam.

This rapid and surprise attack both kept the number of casualties to a minimum and ended the war in a few short hours. Interestingly, all these matters are subtly reflected in the verses of this Surah.

Obviously, neither the horse has a specific exclusivity, nor the issue of the spark resulting from their hooves hitting the desert stones, nor the dust of their feet; what has essentiality is the issue of Jihad, and then its tools, which include all modern war equipment today, just as in verse 60 of Surah al-Anfal, alongside "ribat al-khayl" (trained horses), "power" (quwwah) is mentioned in a general sense.

O Allah! Grant us the success of Jihad and self-sacrifice in the path of Your pleasure. O Lord! The rebellious soul tends toward ingratitude and disbelief; protect us from its dangers. O God! You are aware of the inner and outer secrets of everyone and are informed of our deeds; treat us with Your grace and favor.

Amen, O Lord of the Worlds. End of Surah al-Adiyat.
